title: Endovier and the Champion bargain
summary: Celaena’s release from Endovier is conditional: winning the competition would bind her to the king before eventually earning freedom.
evidence: Throne of Glass, Chapter 3

title: The clock tower’s strange markings
summary: The unusual marks near the clock tower establish an early mystery connected to the hidden forces inside the castle.
evidence: Throne of Glass, Chapter 8

title: The competition and the murders
summary: The Champion competition and the violent deaths occurring inside the castle are not separate problems.
evidence: Throne of Glass, Chapters 10–14

title: A path between worlds
summary: Events in Crescent City establish a direct connection to the world of A Court of Thorns and Roses.
evidence: House of Sky and Breath, Chapter 78
A Court of Thorns and Roses completed

title: The Walking Dead appears in two worlds
summary: A book called The Walking Dead is connected to Wyrdmarks in Throne of Glass and later appears among the rare books in Jesiba’s library. The repeated title is explicit; how the same work crossed worlds remains unexplained.
evidence: Throne of Glass, Chapter 27
House of Earth and Blood completed

title: Could Wyrd and Urd be the same underlying force?
summary: Readers connect Wyrd in Erilea with Urd in Midgard because both ideas are associated with fate, world-shaping power, and similar names. The parallel is compelling, but the books have not confirmed that Wyrd and Urd are identical.
evidence: Throne of Glass, Chapter 27
House of Flame and Shadow completed
Community theory — not confirmed canon

title: A falling star between Erilea and Prythian
summary: Aelin’s fall through worlds passes a winged Fae male and his pregnant mate in a mountain-ringed city. A Court of Silver Flames records a strange red star during the corresponding period, connecting the two scenes.
evidence: Kingdom of Ash, Chapter 99
A Court of Silver Flames, Chapter 62

title: Do Midgard’s shifters descend from Erilea’s Fae?
summary: House of Flame and Shadow describes animal-shifting Fae with elemental magic arriving from another world. Fans connect that description to Erilea’s Fae, who combine animal forms with elemental gifts. The source world is strongly suggested but not named outright in the passage.
evidence: Heir of Fire completed
House of Flame and Shadow completed
Strong inference — source world not explicitly named

title: Lidia’s fire and the name Brannon
summary: Lidia explains that her son Brannon was named for an ancient Fae king from another world whose bloodline carried fire and was associated with sacred stags. Those details deliberately echo Brannon Galathynius and Terrasen.
evidence: Throne of Glass series completed
House of Flame and Shadow, Chapter 94

title: The Book of Breathings reaches Midgard
summary: The Book of Breathings, an important magical text in ACOTAR, is later named among the rare books connected to Jesiba’s library. Its presence establishes another physical object shared across worlds, although its route to Midgard remains unknown.
evidence: A Court of Mist and Fury completed
House of Earth and Blood completed

title: Why does Rhysand look like Ruhn?
summary: When Bryce reaches Prythian, Rhysand’s appearance is familiar enough that she initially connects him with Ruhn. The resemblance is explicit, but whether it signals shared ancestry, parallel bloodlines, or another explanation remains unresolved.
evidence: A Court of Mist and Fury completed
House of Sky and Breath, Chapter 78

title: The Daglan and the Asteri
summary: House of Flame and Shadow identifies the ancient conquerors remembered in Prythian as the Daglan with the parasitic rulers known in Midgard as the Asteri.
evidence: A Court of Silver Flames completed
House of Flame and Shadow completed

title: The Horn and the Dread Trove
summary: The Horn carried by Bryce is revealed to belong with the Mask, Harp, and Crown—the objects Prythian remembers as the Dread Trove.
evidence: A Court of Silver Flames completed
House of Flame and Shadow completed

title: One three-faced goddess across worlds?
summary: Witch traditions in more than one series use threefold divine imagery. Fans question whether these faiths remember the same being under different names, but the books have not confirmed a shared goddess.
evidence: Heir of Fire completed
House of Earth and Blood completed
Community theory — not confirmed canon
